era_context: Late Bronze Age; Egypt under Amenhotep III in the final year(s) of his reign. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I was at its height. Mycenaean Greece was in Late Helladic IIIA. The Shang Dynasty in China continued. This was an era of imperial consolidation and international diplomacy in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign in final year; possible regnal year 37–38
events.description: Amenhotep III was in the final year or years of his reign. The pharaoh's health was likely declining, and succession arrangements were being finalized. His son, Amenhotep IV (later Akhenaten), was designated as successor. The transition of power would occur within the next 1–2 years, marking the beginning of the Amarna Period and a significant shift in Egyptian religious and political policy.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Amenhotep III's chronology

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-attested, but the precise dating of his death is debated. Chronological frameworks place his death c. 1370 BCE, with uncertainty of ±5 years.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's reign at its height
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I was in the later years of his reign, having consolidated Hittite control over much of Anatolia and Syria. The Hittite Empire was at its territorial and political peak. The Great King maintained diplomatic relations with Egypt and other major powers, as evidenced by later diplomatic correspondence (the Amarna Letters).
key_figures: Šuppiluliuma I (Great King of the Hittites)
events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is well-documented, but precise year-by-year dating is uncertain. Chronological frameworks vary by ±5–10 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Amenhotep III's death is contested among Egyptologists, with some scholars placing it in 1372 BCE and others in 1370 BCE or even 1368 BCE. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains one of the most debated issues in ancient history. Additionally, no specific events for 1371 BCE are independently documented in contemporary sources; the events listed represent inferred continuation of broader political activities.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1371 BCE is sparse in terms of year-specific documentation. We rely on royal inscriptions, administrative records, and later compilations to infer the general context. The bias toward Egyptian and Hittite court narratives means that perspectives from smaller polities and non-elite populations are largely absent. Archaeological evidence provides some independent verification but lacks year-level precision. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains contested, with competing frameworks differing by up to 15 years.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III's death and succession of Akhenaten
